ENGNew: Gears, Fire, and the Taste of Honey
changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Gears and Fire and the Taste of Honey"

TextENGNew: Human life is built upon the connections between people.
changeReason: New accurately captures that human life is "built upon/sustained by" connections (営まれている), while old says the world is "filled with people blessed with relationships" which is a different concept.

TextENGNew: But that doesn't mean everyone is connected to everyone else.
Everyone understands intuitively that some stranger on the other side of the globe crying or laughing has no bearing on their own life.
Yet within the extremely limited confines of a neighborhood, the idea that people's lives are intertwined makes perfect sense.
It's entirely possible that one memorable act within a small community could have profound consequences for everyone in it.
Scale that up far enough, and... well, perhaps a heroic speech by a nameless stranger on the other side of the world could influence my life.
changeReason: New correctly includes "heroic speech" (英雄的演説) while old replaces it with "legend," losing the specific reference to a speech.

TextENGNew: Well, it's not always that way.
As I said at the start, the connections between people are, fundamentally, not as significant as the world likes to claim.
Whether the family across the street is having hamburger steak or croquettes for dinner has absolutely no effect on me.
changeReason: New correctly translates ハンバーグ as "hamburger steak" (not "steak") and お向かい as "the family across the street" (not "some household nearby").

TextENGNew: When I put on my shoes, it doesn't matter to anybody whether I put the right one on first or the left.
...This much the average person can understand.
But here is the actual, genuine truth of the matter.@kThe connections between people are far more black and white than that.
It's not just a matter of distance, of being far or near.
For example, let's say that Person A's actions have some effect on me.
Even then, Person B's actions could have absolutely no consequence on my life.
The reverse also holds true. Just because my actions affect Person A, that doesn't always mean that they affect Person B as well.
Let's put it bluntly.
If the bonds between people and their fates can be likened to gears...
changeReason: New includes "fates" (運命) which was omitted from the original translation, changing the scope of the metaphor.

TextENGNew: Then some gears mesh with the one that represents me, and others do not.
There are some who would try arguing against this.
Those people would bring up the example of gears in a clock.
Each gear indeed only directly meshes with one or two others...
But if you turn one gear, it moves the next, which moves the next, and so on -- and in the end, every gear is turning.
...There is a logic behind this, more than enough to convince the average person.
Why is the argument convincing?
...The answer is simple.
The relationships between people are ambiguous, and can only be described conceptually.
Nobody can concretely explain which gears are connected to which, how they interlock, how they relate -- so they have no choice but to obfuscate the matter with that kind of reasoning.
changeReason: Old mistranslates 煙に巻く (obfuscate) as 'throws a wet blanket' (dampen enthusiasm) -- different concept.

TextENGNew: So for the people who like that explanation, I'll use the example of a clock again to refute it.
The fundamental error lies in treating this world as a single clock.
That is, there isn't only one clock.
There exist many clocks in this world, each counting their own time.
Think about it -- the sheer arrogance of assuming this world contains only one clock.
Even if you use the analogy of gears to explain human relationships,@k then you should be able to explain it using an analogy of multiple clocks unrelated to a single gear.
Neighbors A and B.
A is a gear in the same clock as me, so it's best to remain civil.
B is a gear in a different clock, so he doesn't really matter to me at all.
That's the kind of clear distinction I'm talking about.
You want to say I'm being fallacious?
Then let me translate it into something closer to everyday life so you can understand.
You've surely heard the expression "a fire on the far shore," haven't you?
For example, if your neighbor's house was on fire, you'd probably try and help to put it out, wouldn't you?@k It'd be awful if the fire spread and burned down your own house, after all.
But what if that fire was in a town on the opposite side of a river?
Would you really go out of your way to help?@kYou wouldn't, would you?@kThere's no chance whatsoever the fire could spread to your house.
changeReason: Old mistranslates 間違っても (not even by chance) as a moral judgment; new correctly conveys impossibility.

TextENGNew: Even if it turned into a huge conflagration, there's no relation between the houses that will spread fire to yours and those that won't.
With this basic example, you should be able to see the difference between gears that are or aren't related to your own.
...Things like that exist all around us, even without a river to separate them.
changeReason: New correctly translates that such situations "exist all around us" (たくさんある), while old says "a lot to think about" which misrepresents the meaning.

TextENGNew: It's not a question of distance -- not about being neighbors or being across a river, you see?
